  "summary": "This chunk details two major illusion-based rituals from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849): experiment no. 6 for conjuring a phantom banquet with sixteen spirits, and experiment no. 7 for summoning fifteen squire-spirits to create an illusory castle under imperial scrutiny. It includes full conjuration formulas, spirit names, ritual tools (e.g., hoopoe offering, sword-drawn circles), and protective structures. The text emphasizes the deceptive nature of these illusions—food does not nourish, horses are demons in disguise—and notes their limited duration (a quarter-day). Connections to broader magical traditions (Greek papyri, Grail literature, Tibetan Buddhist hagiography) are noted.",
  "key_passages": [
    "‘Oymelor, Demefin, Lamair, Masair, Symofor, Rodobayl, Tentetos, Lotobor, Memoyr, Tamafin, Leutaber, Tatomofon, Faubair, Selutabel, Rimasor, Syrama, most cheerful, glad and joyous spirits, I, so-and-so, adjure you…’",
    "‘the hoopoe is possessed of great virtue for necromancers and invokers of demons [nigromanticis et demones invocantibus], on which account we use it much for our safekeeping.’",
    "‘And when they have sworn, you will give them the hoopoe, and when they have it they will ask your leave to withdraw. You will give it to them, saying, “Go forth wherever you wish to go, and be attentive to me.”’",
    "‘I also wish to explain to you how to obtain a horse – that is, a spirit in the form of a beast…’",
    "‘the creature that appears is not in reality a horse but a demon in the form of a horse.’"
  ]
}
